questions.q: A user reports that their semantic search engine returns score 0.0 for the query 'my app crashes' against a document titled 'Application Failure Troubleshooting'. What is the most likely cause?
options: The embedding model has a bug in the cosine similarity calculation
The search engine is using bag-of-words / lexical matching, not neural embeddings
The document was not indexed before the query was run
The cosine similarity of unrelated texts is always 0.0
questions.explanation: A score of 0.0 when words don't overlap is the signature of bag-of-words / lexical matching. Cosine similarity on a zero vector (no matching words) returns 0.0. Neural embeddings would score this pair ~0.8+ because they capture meaning, not word overlap.

questions.q: Why is cosine similarity preferred over Euclidean distance for comparing text embeddings?
options: Cosine similarity is faster to compute for large vectors
Cosine similarity is insensitive to vector magnitude, which varies with document length
Euclidean distance cannot handle negative vector components
Cosine similarity always produces values in [0, 1] while Euclidean distance can be unbounded
questions.explanation: A long document accumulates more token contributions than a short one, producing a larger-magnitude vector even if both cover the same topic. Cosine similarity measures only the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude — so a long and short document on the same topic can still score near 1.0.

questions.q: You call model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True). What does normalization do to retrieval quality?
options: It improves retrieval quality by scaling scores to [0, 1]
It allows using dot product as a numerically equivalent proxy for cosine similarity
It degrades quality slightly but improves memory usage
It makes all embeddings identical in magnitude, reducing discriminative power
questions.explanation: Normalizing vectors to unit length means |v| = 1 for all vectors. At that point, dot(a, b) = cos(a, b) exactly — no division needed. This is a computational optimization, not a quality change. Rankings are identical to un-normalized cosine similarity.

questions.q: You embed 500 support documents and run sanity checks. The similarity score between 'How to cancel a subscription' and 'Unsubscribe from billing plan' is 0.38. What should you do?
options: This score is fine — anything above 0.3 counts as similar
The cosine similarity formula is implemented incorrectly
The embedding model is likely wrong for this domain; evaluate alternatives from Lesson 02
Increase the number of documents in the corpus to improve embedding quality
questions.explanation: A well-functioning general-purpose embedding model should score obvious paraphrases at 0.8+. A score of 0.38 on a clear paraphrase indicates the model doesn't understand the domain. The fix is to evaluate and select a better-matched model (covered in Lesson 02), not to add more documents.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes what an embedding model is trained to do?
options: Map text to a vector such that the Euclidean distance equals the edit distance between strings
Map text to a vector such that semantically similar strings produce vectors with similar directions
Compress text losslessly into a fixed-length binary representation
Tokenize text and assign a unique integer ID to each token
questions.explanation: Embedding models are trained with contrastive objectives: pull similar text pairs together in embedding space, push dissimilar pairs apart. The result is a geometry where direction encodes meaning. Edit distance and token IDs are completely separate concepts.

questions.q: Your retrieval system's top-1 score for known-good queries is consistently 0.45. Your documents are English, your model is all-MiniLM-L6-v2, and your queries are English. What is the most productive next step?
options: Add more documents to the index to give the model more context
Switch from cosine similarity to Euclidean distance for more discriminative scoring
Inspect a sample of retrieved chunks to check if chunking splits answers across boundaries
Increase the embedding dimension by using a larger model like text-embedding-3-large
questions.explanation: Before changing the model, inspect what is actually being retrieved. A top-1 score of 0.45 often means the correct answer spans a chunk boundary — the best matching chunk contains only part of the answer. Fixing chunking is lower cost and often more impactful than upgrading the embedding model.
